heading: MODULO DECIMO
card_summary: Chunk 120 forms the tenth "module" of Zorzi's Cantico Terzo and develops an elaborate theology of the celestial banquet (convivio) that awaits the blessed at the heavenly nuptials. Zorzi treats the bread, wine, flesh, and fruit served at this eschatological feast through sustained typological exegesis, drawing on John 6, the Wedding at Cana, the Last Supper, Ezekiel's river-vision, and the Apocalypse. Jewish rabbinic tradition is cited for the heavenly banquet's three courses (bull, bird, fish), while the twelve fruits of the Tree of Life from Revelation 22 are mapped onto the twelve apostles and the twelve zodiacal signs. The section concludes with Isaiah 65's vision of the new Jerusalem, the wolf and the lamb eating together, and the serpent's permanent expulsion from the feast, all read as descriptions of the final state of perfect peace and union with Christ.
essay:
# De Harmonia Mundi: Zorzi's Theological Symphonology and Cosmic Union

## 1. The Preparation of the Bread of Life

God, whose works are perfect, like the supreme Artisan made all things very good and, in their respective degrees, perfect. Therefore he, who is also an excellent Provider (as we have observed previously) prepared the food and beverages suitable for the sustenance of both dimensions of man, so that man, deprived of external defense, would not fail toward the supreme Artisan who has accumulated for man so many benefits. Thus he prepared manifold foods to prevent nausea. Yet, and this thing excites wonder, for the animal dimension of man he prepared a single food, namely bread, which never generates fastidiousness, and a single beverage which gives sustenance, life and joy, namely wine, which, by virtue of its solar nature always does good, unless the subject who assumes it is ill-disposed or consumes it in excessive quantity. In the same way for the spiritual dimension of man he prepared a food that has the appearance and name of bread, because there is no natural food more suitable, to which one could compare it and from which to draw the name. Therefore he called bread the heavenly ambrosia and the sweetest manna which is now hidden but which will then be unveiled, a food full of every suavity, saying: *I am the living bread come down from heaven, if any one eats of it, he will live forever*.

What is beautiful and fitting is that the bread of life is prepared for the sustenance and life of the members of Christ so that they might live of the spirit and the life of Christ, so that what the apostle says comes to pass: *All of us, who partake of one bread and one chalice, are one body and one bread*. For just as from many grains of wheat is made one bread, so from the many members of believers is formed the unique body of Christ that lives entirely of the life of Christ, indeed of that life which is Christ. Therefore he said: *He who eats of this bread will live forever*; and again: *He who feeds on me will live because of me*. And as nothing transforms itself better nor in a more fitting way into our body than bread, so that we might be transformed into Christ, we are given this food, and he who eats it does not transform it into himself, but is transformed into the food he has eaten (Augustine says this) in order to live in it.

Zorzi's theology of the Eucharistic transformation operates within a sophisticated Neoplatonic framework that sees the material and spiritual as interpenetrating dimensions of a unified cosmos. The bread-body analogy draws on medieval sacramental theology, particularly the tradition of Aquinas and Bonaventure, but Zorzi pushes toward a more radical understanding of transformation that echoes both Origen's theory of consumption and deification and the Kabbalistic concept of devekuth (cleaving to God). The "transformation" he describes is not merely psychological or moral reform, but an ontological restructuring wherein the communicant's substance participates in Christ's divine nature. This participatory soteriology—rooted in patristic theology but systematized through Neoplatonic emanationism—suggests that eating the bread is not a metaphorical appropriation of grace but an actual communion of substances mediated through Christ as the incarnate Logos. Zorzi's language of "transmutation" (transmutatio) deliberately echoes both alchemical and Aristotelian metaphysics, suggesting that the bread undergoes a transition from one substantial form to another in the digestive process of grace.

The spiritual efficacy of the Eucharist depends, moreover, upon what Zorzi elsewhere calls "concordance" with divine will. Just as the bread must be consumed with synchronization of appetite and satisfaction, so the communicant must approach the sacrament in a state of proper disposition. This requirement echoes both the Pauline dictum about eating unworthily and the apophatic mysticism of Pseudo-Dionysius, for whom negative theology prepares the soul for union. Zorzi's insistence that the bread cannot sustain those "deprived of external defense" suggests that the sacrament requires a prior stripping away of false supports—wealth, reputation, earthly security. The person who would eat must have nowhere else to turn, no defense but Christ himself. This creates a phenomenology of poverty that grounds mystical theology in lived experience rather than abstract speculation.

Therefore, whoever desires to have a place where he might live and the means for living: let him believe, let him enter, let him eat, let him incorporate himself, transformed, and having received in gift a new life so that he might one day eat that new food and drink that new wine; those who shall have tasted of it will not be subject either to bodily or to spiritual death. That is the true bread of life and the authentic manna hidden, which no one knows save he who receives it, therefore it is not possible to be instructed on its account by others save from experience and the taste that the Dispenser of grace offers.

## 2. The Wine of the Wedding Feast

At the last supper he gave us a marvelous wine and has promised us a better one to taste in the harmonious kingdom and at the celestial wedding, saying: *Henceforth I will not drink of this fruit of the vine until the day when I drink it new in the kingdom of my Father*. Since we are treating of that kingdom and of those weddings, we must explain what that wine is which he promises to give there. Now, wine is extracted from the pressing of grapes and is born of the vine; nor can it come from another vine save from that of which he said: *I am the true vine*. It is prepared in those celestial weddings, symbolized by those others in which the heavenly Spouse showed himself for the first time superior to man and the order of nature, changing water into wine in an instant and at mere command while nature can do it only with a considerable interval of time and with appropriate means. Now, so that those weddings, which he had decided to consecrate in the mystery of the authentic weddings, would not become sad and squalid for the lack of wine, the most merciful mother, feeling compassion for human weakness and poverty, said to the Son, that is, to the true Vine: *They have no wine*. Yet, since Jesus was not at the authentic weddings but as a guest at the wedding feast of others he responded: *What is that to me and to you, woman?* These are not our weddings. Not yet has my hour come, in which the authentic weddings will be prepared for me, the true Spouse. The mother, however, having at heart also the figurative weddings, with maternal authority solicitous for their needs and conscious of the will of the Son, said to the servants: *Do whatever he tells you*. The evangelist adds, conscious of celestial mysteries: *There were in that place six water jars*. These jars are interpreted by Jerome in his commentary on Ezekiel and by other commentators in other passages as the six ages of life, like vessels capable of containing both supramundane and inferior waters. This water, if it passes through the true Vine which is Christ, for the effect of the heating of the supramundane Sun, is transformed into good wine, according to the significance of that figurative transmutation. But if the jars are full of only inferior water, that is, of graces conceded, as it is said, freely conceded, on account of the boiling of the evil heat of concupiscence they are transformed often into wine, of which Moses in an indignant tone says in his canticle: *Their vine is from the vine of Sodom, and from the fields of Gomorrah, their grapes are grapes of poison, their clusters are bitter. Their wine is the poison of serpents, and the cruel venom of asps*. With that wine the great harlot, that is, worldly concupiscence, has made all the gentiles drink. Therefore it is said that wine comes from the vineyards of Sodom and from the fields of Gomorrah. Those cities, as they burned with their most evil desires, so they burned also with the fire of divine justice. This is the vineyard of lovers of the world, whose grapes and clusters are most bitter gall because it is bitter to have abandoned the Lord on account of desires. Therefore one must weep twice bitterly, as Jeremiah says in the Lamentations addressing his people wholly imprisoned by worldly passions: *Make a bitter mourning for an only son, a most bitter lamentation*. Ezekiah, for his part, realizing where that wine had led him, said: *I will recount for you all my years in bitterness of soul*. It is fitting that this worldly wine be called the gall of serpents since it was offered by the ancient serpent for our irreparable ruin, so much so that immediately after it is said and the cruel poison of asps, that is, incurable by human forces without the aid of the heavenly Redeemer. To this wine corresponds that most bitter wine of divine judgment, of which the prophet in the psalm says: *In the hand of the Lord is a cup of wine foaming full of mixture, or of commixture, because the Lord has authority, judgment, power and justice to reward the good and punish sinners*. He made it still, or overflow, while our translation has 'he inclined it'. He poured and still pours malice upon the head of he who commits evil, according to the decree of divine justice. Certainly all the wicked of the earth will drink of it to the dregs: the good, indeed, taste the wine of divine justice, while sinners and the wicked drink its dregs. But let us return to those who drink the good wine here and who will be refreshed one day with that better and heavenly one.

Zorzi's distinction between the wine of charity-and-wisdom and the wine of divine judgment reveals his sophisticated understanding of the relationship between grace and justice within divine action. Drawing on the Kabbalistic tradition of the Sefirot—particularly the interplay of Gevurah (severity/justice) and Chesed (mercy)—Zorzi presents an ontological unity that preserves the apparent contradiction between divine clemency and divine wrath. The six water jars themselves correspond to the six directions of the supernal configuration (four cardinal directions plus above and below), a spatial-mystical geometry that echoes both Platonic cosmology and Kabbalistic visualization practices. The transformation of water into wine via passage through the true Vine is not merely a moral allegory but a genuine alchemy of essences wherein the inferior is elevated toward participation in the divine solar quality.

The contrast between Sodomitic wine and heavenly wine illustrates what might be called Zorzi's "inverted alchemy." Just as the wise man transforms natural passion into spiritual love through the heat of divine fire, so the fool transforms the gifts of grace into instruments of self-destruction through the fever of concupiscence. Both employ "heat" or fermentation, but one produces ascent toward God while the other produces descent toward the serpent. This binary structure, repeated throughout De Harmonia Mundi, suggests that spiritual transformation is not the elimination of human desire but its radical reorientation—the same energies that produce Sodomitic wine can, through proper guidance and divine grace, produce the wine of celestial ecstasy. The problem is not eros itself but misdirected eros.

The wine that we must drink here is of a twofold nature: that of charity and of wisdom, not surely that of superior wisdom, which in sacred Scripture is called milk, as we have declared elsewhere, which is given to few, but wine to many. And to this also we are invited in the Song where it is said: *Drink, friends, and become inebriated*; and again through the prophet: *How glorious is my cup that inebriates*. That cup, offered and poured out on the day of Pentecost, so inebriated the disciples that some of those who stood by said that they were drunk on new wine. Certainly they were drunk, and full of that wine which truly gladdens the heart of man, which in Greek speech is called Paraclete, that is, consoler. Peter, after having tasted a little of that wine on Mount Tabor, was so full of joy that he said: *Lord, it is good for us to be here*. Paul too had tasted of it, for he was saying: *I desire to depart and be with Christ*, so that I might drink that better and new wine in the house of his Father. Stephen too had tasted of the sweetness of it, whereof drunk, the stones of the torrent with which he was stoned were to him sweet. The prophet too had drunk, who, making libations, cries out: *Taste and see that the Lord is good*. The apostles and all the martyrs had tasted of it, so that made wholly drunk and possessed like bacchants, they might hasten to death most atrocious so as to drink more quickly that better one, to which they were urged by the sweetness of this inferior. Wine is also the blood of the Lamb which has been poured out for us. The Lamb himself invites all to drink of it, saying: *Take and drink of it all of you. This is the cup of my blood, of the new and eternal testament, which is poured out for you and for many unto the remission of sins*. *Do this in commemoration of me*, and in memory of the better wine prepared in the house of the eternal Father. So that you might be capable of drinking it, behold I turn this wine into my blood, full of that life which I give together with blood so that you too might live of that life by virtue of which you are able to drink that wine which no one drinks save he who lives of celestial life.

This is the sense of his words: *I dispose, by my passion and the pouring out of my blood, a kingdom for you, as my Father has disposed one for me*, in such a way that through this bitter passion and this transformation into another manner of living I may come to that banquet in which is drunk the wine of perfect beatitude flowing from the true Bacchus and true Sun. Therefore he said to the apostles who desired to sit with him: *Are you able to drink the cup which I am about to drink?*, indicating clearly enough that whoever desires to have access to that celestial banquet must drink the cup of passion, denying himself, and transforming himself through the true death of the cross, so that he might follow Jesus going that way to that banquet. These, therefore, are the cups and these are the wines we must drink here: wisdom, charity, mortification, and the transformation of oneself in celestial life so that we might drink the wine to be placed there and celebrate true bacchanalias and engage in true rejoicing and give and receive the true nuptial embraces where two, that is, every blessed creature, is united in one spirit with Christ and God to whom it has adhered, from whom it can by no means be separated. Then it will be permitted to fix true kisses and to enjoy all delights with impunity, since nothing is forbidden and nothing is prohibited for those who are joined in true and most holy union, because in it there is no vice to which the blessed, who will be (as Augustine says) perfect in very peaceful virtue and condition, might yield or be subject.

## 3. Which Bread and Which Flesh

Since above we have spoken of bread alone, now it remains to examine how the bread made flesh is given to us at the banquet of celestial weddings, according to the words of the Spouse: *The bread which I will give is my flesh for the life of the world*. What is the significance of his words according to which the bread is flesh? Bread, indeed, is eaten so that it is transformed into flesh and becomes flesh. Since, on the other hand, this celestial bread is not transformed but transforms he who eats of it, we eat of this bread so that we might be changed into the flesh and blood of Christ and from two we become one flesh so that the true Adam might say of us: *Finally bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh*. We eat of this bread so that we might be transformed into the body and flesh of Christ when we receive his teaching, which is bread of life and of understanding, and is the water of saving wisdom, but also when we are nourished in due manner of the body itself of Christ. However, none of these things ferments and is transmuted unless that fire of the Holy Spirit and of love is present, which cooks the food before we receive it, in such a way that it does not happen to us to be nourished of it from habit, hypocrisy, or from some other perverse cause, but from sincere love and affection. By that fire, moreover, we heat and digest the food we have received. Then indeed true transformation will be accomplished in us by virtue of the fire of love, which (as the academics, the epicureans, but especially our Dionysius teaches) transforms the lover into the beloved. Therefore the flesh prepared in a special manner for the celestial weddings is the flesh of our Spouse and of the Immaculate Lamb immolated for the life of the world.

Zorzi's treatment of the eucharistic flesh as simultaneously personal and universal—the flesh of the Spouse and the flesh of the universal Church—reflects the medieval distinction between the "natural body" (corpus naturale) and the "mystical body" (corpus mysticum) of Christ, but recasts it through Neoplatonic hylomorphism. The transformation effected by the fire of the Holy Spirit is not merely forensic or juridical (as in some Protestant accounts of justification) but substantial and ontological. The person who receives the sacrament undergoes a metabolic transformation wherein their entire being becomes consubstantial with Christ. This echoes the Eastern Orthodox understanding of theosis or deification, wherein the human participates in divine nature without losing human nature. Zorzi's insistence on the necessity of sincere love (syncero amore) as the condition for true transformation introduces an ethical dimension: the sacrament's efficacy depends not merely on ritual performance but on the communicant's interior disposition and authentic eros toward God.

The distinction Zorzi draws between consuming and being consumed is crucial. The normal operation of digestive metabolism is reversed or inverted in the eucharistic mystery: instead of the bread being destroyed and incorporated into the body of the eater, the eater is dissolved and incorporated into the eternal body of Christ. This reversal encodes a fundamental theological truth—that salvation consists not in the assertion of the ego but in its dissolution into divine love. The fire of the Holy Spirit is presented as the agent of this inversion, burning away the false self so that only the divine self remains. This carries profound implications for Christian anthropology: it suggests that the human person is not a closed system of autonomous will but rather a potential dwelling-place for the infinite.

Beyond this flesh, in the Gospel where the celestial weddings themselves are being treated, the Spouse makes mention of certain other fleshes, saying: *My oxen and my fatted beasts are already slaughtered*, where (according to Jerome) the oxen symbolize the magnitude of the dogmas and the fatted beasts the profundity of the teaching of God. The Hebrew sages, however, affirm that God prepares for the just a banquet in which will be served an ox, a bird, and a fish: to the first of these Christ makes reference. The ox, indeed, is our animal dimension which was being purified by means of the sacrificial ox prescribed by the law. The bird is the supreme portion of the soul which, in its flight, always tends toward lofty places and is interpreted by Jerome in another passage as the eagle. The fish, finally, which finds its nourishment in waters, is our middle spirit or our rational dimension, which is nourished and delighted in the waters of wisdom and of doctrines. All these viands are prepared in that banquet to be eaten by the Spouse and by his table companions, as he himself says in the Apocalypse: *I will dine with them and they with me*. He takes delight in dining with us, but not because he has need of our goods but because, in the act of eating, he might transform us into himself, so that we become one thing with him, after having been eaten by him, digested, and changed into his very body.

The tripartite division of human nature—animal (ox), spirited (bird), and rational (fish)—derives from Platonic psychology as mediated through Philo, Origen, and medieval Scholasticism. Yet Zorzi inflects this threefold anthropology toward a distinctly Neoplatonic interpretation wherein each component participates according to its own mode in the transcendent principle. The ox represents the bodily appetites and sensory powers; the bird, the will and emotional intellection; the fish, the rational intellect immersed in the living waters of divine wisdom. Each must be transformed in the eucharistic banquet, each must be subjected to the consuming fire of divine love. This comprehensive soteriology—addressing the whole human person rather than merely the rational soul—stands in sharp contrast to Gnostic dualism and affirms instead the Christianized Platonism of the Patristic fathers.

The mention of fish warrants particular exegetical attention. Throughout Scripture and Patristic tradition, the fish carries associations with both cosmic fecundity (the waters being the matrix of creation) and hidden or submerged wisdom. In the Kabbalistic schema, the fish corresponds to the lower Sefirot, particularly Yesod (foundation), which mediates between the supernal triad and the material world. Thus Zorzi's inclusion of fish in the eucharistic banquet suggests that even the intermediate dimensions of consciousness—the imagination, the subconscious will, the subtle intuitions—are drawn into redemptive transformation. Nothing is left untouched by grace.

The matter of the fish is further elucidated by Ezekiel, when he says: *His fish will be of diverse kinds, like the fish of the Great Sea*, because very numerous will be, not only the kinds, but also the genera of those fish, that is, the spirits and rational souls: those spirits will be admitted to that banquet to be united with the spouse. Christ did not name those fish when speaking of the banquet prepared, but, after having risen and having assumed the nature and condition of spirit, willed to eat thereof, giving also to his disciples, according to what John narrates: *Jesus took the bread and gave it to them, and so also the fish*. Luke too says: *When he had eaten a portion of roasted fish and a honeycomb, taking the remains, he gave them to them, saying: These are the words which I spoke to you when I was still with you*. The fish which Jesus eats after the resurrection, therefore, is different from those born in the sea, since (according to the testimony of Christ) his remains are the words which Jesus spoke to them when he was among humans. The spiritual words, indeed, are the remains of the spirit itself which we must keep with far greater reverence than the relics of the saints, in which their spirits also dwelt.

## 4. What is the Fruit of this Banquet

Our singers Ezekiel and John describe also the fruits prepared for this banquet. Ezekiel, indeed, says: *On the banks of the river that passes through the city, there is every kind of fruit tree: their leaves do not wither and their fruits will not fail. Every month they will produce new fruits because their waters come from the sanctuary, and their fruits are food and their leaves medicine*. The words of John echo those of Ezekiel but are more explicit, perhaps because his vision was clearer. He says: *In the middle of the plaza of the city and along the two banks of the river is the tree of life, which bears fruit twelve times, and its leaves are for the healing of the nations*. These words conceal deep mysteries and far weightier than our weak shoulders can bear, however, (with the aid of God) we will attempt to say something of them. They agree in mentioning first the river that passes through the city, perhaps it is the same one of which the royal prophet speaks: *The force of the river gladdens the city of God*. This river represents the confluence of all goods that flow forth from the incessant Source from which all good proceeds: it gladdens the city itself of God and all its inhabitants. The tree of life is that from which they receive the promised and eternal life. What those fruits are which are said to mature every month, O good God, if you, who have planted the tree, did not teach us, I would not dare to say anything. They do not seem to me to coincide with the fruits of the Spirit of which Paul speaks, because they are destined to be tasted by him who is still on his way and not to be served at the heavenly nuptial banquet. Now, if that is the tree of life, which is the Son, it would be more fitting to say that those are fruits of the Son, rather than of the Spirit, although they might be said to be fruits of the spirit because they are spiritual and grow by virtue of the power of the Spirit. Nonetheless the tree is the Son himself of God who, planted in the flesh of earthly nature, has produced every kind of fruit. Yet the text mentions twelve particular fruits because they are the principal fruits and it is affirmed that they mature in individual months. Now, in order to understand what the months and days allude to (since in that eternal succession there is no distinction of years, days, or months) a different sense must be sought, different from what the letter sounds, in which the monthly production of those fruits might be verified.

The introduction of temporal language (months, days) into the description of eternal reality raises profound hermeneutical and metaphysical questions. Zorzi resolves this through a Neoplatonic framework wherein temporal succession in the created cosmos reflects the eternal processions and returns within the supernal realm. Just as the Platonic Forms constitute the intelligible paradigms toward which temporal things aspire, so the "months" of the celestial banquet represent eternal archetypal divisions or hypostatic distinctions within the divine mind. This is not pantheism but rather a carefully calibrated doctrine of participated being wherein creation perpetually mirrors and reflects its transcendent source.

We have said the twelve apostles are placed over both the figurative and the true family, which are subdivided in correspondence with the twelve celestial signs, through whose spaces the sun marks the twelve months. Therefore, the months in those eternal dwellings are not referred to the scansion of time but to the distributive partition of space, figured by the temporal scansion here below as causes are figured by their effects or authentic forms by pictorial representations. Now, since the apostles will rule those twelve families, of which the marked and the principal exponents are characterized by the number twelve thousand, so also the principal fruits of the tree of life are twelve in correspondence with the months: they are the principal fruits which the Tree of life has produced for the nourishment and guidance of the entire family. We have already explained that the Prince himself has been exposed as food and nourishment and the same holds for all of us. The leaves, then, offered for the healing of the nations are those who preach for the good of others and not for their own gain, as we have already observed, or they are the sacraments offered for the health of those who are wounded or sick on account of original sin or of actual sin. The leaves, indeed, are a kind of tegument that symbolizes the function of the sacraments which purify sins and cover the nakedness of sinners. Yet those leaves of the fig tree could not cover the nakedness of Adam because they had not received their power from the tree of life, nor were they leaves of that tree. Indeed, if Adam had taken of its leaves, perhaps he would have been able to cover his own nakedness. But God forbade it, saying: *Lest he take of the tree of life*, because it was not yet time to distribute its fruits or its leaves, but when the fullness of time came, the leaves of this tree have been distributed and the sacraments have been given for the health of all the sick, provided they are disposed by the due contrition and conversion to God, as he himself says by the mouth of the prophet: *So that I may heal those contrite of heart*. This is done always by means of the power of that tree which is life, light, and Word, of which the psalmist says: *He sent his word and healed them*. In these twelve fruits are comprehended all the others, whatever they may be, as above the twelve foundations (we have explained) all other things are built. And just as those twelve foundations rest on a single foundation, which is Christ Jesus, so all those fruits emanate from the unique Tree of life which is Christ Jesus, irrigated by the water that flows from the Sanctuary from which every good is given and every perfect gift, and from which the very Tree of life declares having received all when it says: *The Father has given me all things*. Of those fruits the Spouse will delight, and the Father supreme as well, considering them like a scion of the most beloved Son. All those fruits, moreover, will congratulate one another considering that they proceed from such a tree and have been served at so noble a nuptial banquet; they, indeed (and this excites wonder), will not be only viands but also table companions, and from their consumption will result no diminution.

## 5. Which the Abundance and the Sweetness of the Foods

Foods would not be adequate unless they were abundant, nor good unless they were savory. These two qualities are revealed (in the opinion of Augustine) by the divine player of the cithara when he says: *They will regurgitate for the remembrance of the abundance of your sweetness*. Those foods, indeed, are so sweet and abundant and are devoured with such avidity that the table companions will not be ashamed to regurgitate the superabundant portions which they continually receive. Some, to be sure, translate with 'they will speak' rather than 'regurgitate', but the latter translation is better, since the verbal form yabbi'u derives from the root naba', which means 'to abound' and 'to regurgitate', as the Seventy also understood it, to whom Augustine adheres. Therefore those most savory and abundant foods must be eaten in great quantity, since the regurgitation derived from the abundance of food is extolled by the prophet to the praise of the table companions. Moreover, we are invited to become inebriated by the Spouse of the celestial weddings, who says in the Song: *Drink, dearest ones, and become inebriated*, since to you is given a measure good, pressed down, and overflowing, as is read in the Gospel according to Luke. For just as the gluttony of bodily foods is condemned as unworthy, so the abundance, drunkenness, and gorging of spiritual foods is recommended as something precious and worthy of praise. Therefore the blessed who eat in that region are lauded while fasting is condemned, according to the words of the Prince of that banquet which are read in Isaiah: *My servants will eat and you will hunger; behold my servants will drink and you will thirst*, you who have committed evil in my sight. A little further on he declares openly when these things will occur, saying: *Behold, I create a new heaven and a new earth, and the former things will not be remembered*, because there will be a new life, a new condition, and a new abode, about which he adds a little later, saying: *Behold I create Jerusalem, the supernal one, namely, where those weddings will be celebrated in exultation and his people will be in joy*. *No more will be heard in it a voice of weeping and a voice of crying*. Then the wolf and the lamb will pasture together because from both sides (the right from which the lamb comes and the left from which the wolf comes) they will participate together in the banquet, and the tribunal of justice and that of mercy will be one, after those have been driven out who, deferred to the universal judgment, will be placed on the left to be condemned on account of their faults. After the pronouncement of that sentence no longer will there go forth from the tribunal of justice any execution of judgment to punish the wicked, therefore it continues: *The lion will eat hay together with the ox*, and no more flesh in punishment of those who have rebelled against their Creator. For the serpent, expelled irrevocably from that banquet and from that dwelling, there will remain as food dust, as the prophet says: *The serpent will feed on dust*, as it was told to it from the beginning: *Dust you will eat all the days of your life*. Since perfect peace will reign and union with the Prince, all the ferocity of every noxious animal and all punitive justice will be filled with exultation, and therefore it follows: *They will not harm nor will they kill on all my holy mountain, says the Lord*, but will be full of joy at that banquet and eternal convivium.

Zorzi's vision of the new creation in this final section synthesizes eschatological hope with a profound recasting of cosmic reconciliation. The remarkable image of the wolf and lamb pasturing together, drawn from Isaiah, is reinterpreted not as mere symbolic peace but as an ontological transformation wherein the predatory natures of creatures are fundamentally altered. This reflects a Stoic-Neoplatonic understanding of the cosmic pneuma gradually achieving perfect rationality throughout creation. The tribunal of justice becoming one with the tribunal of mercy suggests a reconciliation of what had appeared as opposition: the divine attributes achieve their ultimate synthesis in the eschaton. Even the serpent, expelled from the banquet yet preserved as a conscious being, retains its ancient punishment—dust—which Zorzi interprets not as annihilation but as a form of diminishment corresponding to its choices.

The "new life, new condition, and new abode" that Zorzi repeatedly emphasizes echoes Paul's language of being "raised with Christ" and indwelling the heavenly places, but Zorzi grounds this resurrection eschatology in a participatory metaphysics. The new creation is not imposed upon the old as an external renovation but emerges from the eternal transformation of all creatures into Christ. The Jerusalem that is created (future tense yet eternal) represents the perfected communio sanctorum wherein every rational creature has been transmuted through love into a dwelling-place of divine presence. This celestial city is not separate from creation but rather creation achieved—the material and spiritual orders unified and harmonized in the music of the spheres that is the title of this very work.

The emphasis on abundance (copia), satiety, and inebriation as spiritual goods directly inverts ascetic renunciation as the sole path to sanctity. While Zorzi does not reject mortification—indeed, he includes it among the wines one must drink—he insists that the telos of ascetical practice is not perpetual denial but rather a capacity to receive and enjoy infinite goods without destructive excess. The blessed do not merely subsist in heaven; they revel, they gorge themselves, they become "drunk" on divine sweetness. This rehabilitates the body, sensory pleasure, and appetite as eschatological realities rather than mere obstacles to be overcome. It is a vision fundamentally at odds with Manichean or purely spiritualist soteriology, affirming instead the resurrection of the body and the sanctification of matter itself.
